ROGII — Wells on the Geology

Companion to geological_map.html: this report paints the 773 training horizontal trajectories on top of the interpolated geology, and visualises how lateral length, mean TVT and mean GR are distributed across the play. Generated by report/generate_wells_on_geology.py.

1. Headline Numbers

Lateral length (median)
6,575 ft (1.25 mi)
Lateral length p5–p95
4,651 – 8,613 ft
Mean TVT (median across wells)
11,327 ft
Mean GR (median across wells)
86.6 API
Eagle Ford thickness (median)
162 ft
Wells / test wells
773 / 3

2. Wellbores on the Geological Background

Four overlays, all on the same XY base map. Each thin colored line is one training well's horizontal trajectory from heel to toe (downward triangle). The three black lines with white stars are the visible test wells.

  • (a) Eagle Ford TOP depth as backdrop. Lateral length encoded in well color. Longer wells (yellow-green) are spread across the deeper south-central portion of the play; shorter wells cluster more sparsely.
  • (b) Eagle Ford THICKNESS as backdrop. Mean TVT per well encoded in color. The redder/bluer wells track the local Eagle Ford depth — wells that drill the deeper Eagle Ford have correspondingly deeper mean TVT.
  • (c) Structural dip as backdrop. Mean GR encoded in color. Wells with higher mean GR (yellow on cividis) cluster in particular sub-regions — there's a real spatial pattern in lithology, not just random noise.
  • (d) Toe density heatmap. Heels (cyan circles) and toes (black triangles) are not uniformly distributed — there are well-pad clusters where many laterals share a heel and fan out, and broader regions where drilling is sparser.
The takeaway: Drilling is structured, not random. Wells avoid the shallowest and deepest extremes; they target a relatively narrow Eagle Ford depth window; they often share a pad (so the heel positions are clustered). Per Insight 3 in our knowledge base, neighbours are physically relevant — this view makes it visible.

3. Distributions

Lateral length distribution is right-skewed: most wells are ~5,500–7,500 ft laterals, with a tail of very long wells extending to ~10,000+ ft. Mean TVT is tightly distributed (the field has a coherent target horizon). Lateral azimuth is bimodal at roughly NE–SW and NW–SE — the classic field-wide drilling pattern oriented perpendicular to the regional max-horizontal-stress direction (for optimal fracture-network exposure).

4. Lateral Length and Geology Relationships

Sampling the interpolated geology at each well's heel gives a per-well "local geology" feature. Three scatter plots:

  • Mean TVT vs local Eagle Ford depth — strong negative slope: wells in regions where the Eagle Ford is deep have deeper mean TVT. This is the "geological coordinate ≈ depth coordinate" relationship that a linear-Z baseline exploits.
  • Lateral length vs local reservoir thickness — relatively flat: drillers don't make laterals proportional to reservoir thickness; lateral length is mostly driven by lease geometry and pad geometry.
  • Mean GR vs local reservoir thickness — weak relationship. The GR variability dominantly comes from where within the Eagle Ford the lateral was steered, not from the bulk thickness available.

5. What This Adds On Top of geological_map.html

geological_map.html showed the geology without wells; this report adds the actual drilling to that picture. Together:

  • The geology surface is interpolated from formation-top observations sampled along the wellbores themselves — so the interpolation is most reliable where wells are densest (panel d).
  • Test wells (white stars in panels a–c, black trajectories in all four) fall in well-sampled regions — no extrapolation risk.
  • The clear visual structure (parallel laterals, two azimuth clusters, clustered pads, single-zone landing) confirms that the dataset comes from one coherent operational area and the same operator(s).
